case_name: River-water potable reuse polishing for pesticide residues and radium under pilot-scale and remote-site constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for river-water treatment to potable reuse quality when tebufenozide, acetochlor degradates, and radium must be controlled under pilot-scale, capital, and remote-location constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A remote inland utility is piloting a compact potable reuse train that uses river water as a blending and augmentation source for a small drinking water system. Monitoring has identified seasonal pesticide-related contaminants from agricultural runoff together with measurable radium contributed by upstream groundwater-fed baseflow and sediment interaction. Several treatment routes are technically plausible, but the preferred option must balance limited pilot capital, low staffing at the remote site, avoidance of difficult chemical supply chains, and the need to demonstrate stable potable-quality effluent rather than simply maximize removal at any cost.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should consistently achieve potable-reuse quality with combined radium controlled below drinking-water standards and pesticide-related contaminants reduced to low, stable levels suitable for public-health protection and permitting.
secondary_goals: keep process train compatible with pilot-only deployment
minimize operator intervention at a remote site
avoid highly complex concentrate or radioactive residual handling where possible
maintain resilience to seasonal turbidity and organic matter swings

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: remote site should avoid large volumes of difficult brine and should minimize specialized handling of radium-bearing solids, though periodic managed disposal is acceptable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system should tolerate river-water variability and operate reliably with infrequent operator attendance

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter to stabilize downstream adsorption and ion exchange performance
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish particulates and protect GAC and resin
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb hydrophobic pesticide compounds including tebufenozide and provide partial removal of pesticide degradate-associated organics
core_unit_functions.selective_radium_ion_exchange: remove dissolved radium to below combined-radium compliance targets
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier appropriate for potable reuse polishing

applicability_rationale: this train balances the mixed contaminant problem by separating organic-micropollutant control from radium control rather than forcing one high-cost unit to do everything
conventional clarification and filtration are well matched to variable river water and reduce DOC loading that would otherwise shorten GAC life
GAC is a practical pilot-scale barrier for tebufenozide and can help with a broad suite of trace organics likely to matter in potable reuse demonstrations
radium ion exchange is a known targeted approach for dissolved radium and is generally less energy-intensive than membranes
compared with membrane-heavy alternatives, this route better fits remote operation and avoids continuous concentrate generation
potential_risks: acetochlor degradates are more polar than parent pesticides and may not be removed as strongly by GAC as more hydrophobic compounds
radium-selective resin or media will require monitored replacement and managed disposal of radium-bearing residuals
if DOC increases sharply during storm events, GAC run length may shorten and pilot data will need to confirm replacement frequency

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and lower oxidant demand
core_unit_functions.ozonation: transform susceptible organic micropollutants and improve downstream biodegradability
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb and biologically polish oxidized organics including some pesticide degradates

applicability_rationale: this route is attractive because acetochlor degradates can be less amenable to adsorption alone, and ozone plus BAC may broaden control across more polar organics
BAC offers a combined adsorption and biologically active polishing step that can be useful in potable reuse pilots with mixed trace-organic concerns
radium still requires a dedicated dissolved-ion removal step, making the train technically sound for the full contaminant set
it ranks below the top option because ozone systems, off-gas safety, and tighter process monitoring increase operational burden at a remote pilot site
potential_risks: ozonation adds mechanical and control complexity that may strain low-staff remote operation
bromate formation risk must be checked if bromide is present even at modest levels
BAC performance can be variable during start-up and requires biological process management

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove bulk suspended matter and some organic matter
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide flexible intermittent adsorption for pesticide-related organics

applicability_rationale: PAC can be attractive at pilot scale because dosing equipment may have lower upfront cost than full GAC contactors
the approach offers operational flexibility during seasonal pesticide peaks rather than requiring continuously loaded fixed beds
a dedicated radium removal step preserves technical feasibility for the radiological requirement
this option ranks lower because remote handling of carbon slurry, dose optimization, solids capture, and spent solids management can become labor-intensive
potential_risks: PAC effectiveness on more persistent degradates may be inconsistent without careful contact time and dose control
additional solids handling can complicate a remote pilot and generate radium-associated waste if process residuals become mixed
performance may vary with storm-driven DOC spikes

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide broad rejection of divalent ions including radium and partial to strong rejection of many organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration is technically capable of addressing the mixed contaminant challenge in a compact barrier process and can be very effective for radium
it may also provide strong co-benefits for hardness and other dissolved constituents relevant to potable reuse quality

despite this technical strength, it ranks lower because concentrate handling at a remote site is difficult, membrane systems require more skilled attention, and capital intensity is high for a pilot-only deployment
potential_risks: brine or concentrate management is a major challenge in remote inland locations
membrane fouling risk is significant for variable river water unless pretreatment is carefully managed
pilot economics may not represent full-scale feasibility because concentrate disposal can dominate lifecycle cost

core_unit_functions.lime_softening_clarification: precipitate hardness and co-remove radium with softening solids
core_unit_functions.recarbonation: stabilize pH and finished water chemistry after softening

applicability_rationale: lime softening is a recognized radium control approach and can work well where hardness is high enough to support co-precipitation
pairing with GAC can make the route technically capable for the full contaminant list

however, the option ranks last because chemical-intensive operation, sludge production, pH control demands, and operator attention are poorly aligned with a remote pilot under capital and staffing constraints
potential_risks: high sludge generation complicates disposal logistics and may create radium-bearing solids requiring careful management
chemical feed reliability and process control are challenging at a remote site
pilot operation may be unstable if source-water hardness and alkalinity vary seasonally

preferred_rank_pattern: hybrid trains that separate organics removal from radium-specific removal should rank near the top
membrane options may appear as technically strong but should not rank first under pilot-only, capital, and remote-location constraints
chemical- and sludge-intensive conventional radium removal routes should rank below simpler adsorption and ion-exchange combinations

must_reflect: pilot-only deployment favors modular and lower-complexity equipment
remote location penalizes options needing intensive chemical logistics or specialist oversight
capital constraint should downgrade membrane and large softening systems even when technically capable
residuals discussion should distinguish between periodic media disposal and continuous concentrate or sludge burdens

must_contain: why GAC or BAC is relevant for pesticide and trace-organic control in river water
why radium generally requires a dedicated dissolved-ion removal mechanism such as ion exchange, softening, or membranes
why acetochlor degradates can be harder to remove by adsorption alone than more hydrophobic parent compounds
why membrane concentrate handling or softening sludge management lowers rank in this scenario

should_mention: seasonal DOC and turbidity impacts on adsorption performance
uncertainty in removal of more polar pesticide degradates
